trial_data.other_data: 3 complete responses and 12 partial responses documented

tier_1_regimens.notes: CAP is the preferred first-line regimen for thymoma based on response data. In the RYTHMIC prospective database study, CAP was associated with a higher objective response rate than other regimens (44% vs 17%, as exclusive systemic therapy) for advanced thymoma or thymic carcinoma [158]. Patients with thymoma should be evaluated clinically for signs of myasthenia gravis and other paraneoplastic syndromes with appropriate workup and treatment.

The regimen has demonstrated efficacy in the treatment of thymic carcinomas, but it is not preferred due to its toxicity profile. Non-anthracycline regimens may be useful for patients who cannot tolerate the preferred regimen [62].

This regimen can also be used in combination with RT as definitive concurrent chemoradiation. Patients with untreated brain metastases or major standard contraindications to antiangiogenics were excluded from the RELEVENT or S1701 study populations.

There is no published experience using this as a preoperative therapy. Patients with untreated brain metastases or major standard contraindications to antiangiogenics were excluded from the study. The NCCN Panel notes there is no published experience using this as a preoperative therapy [8,197].

tier_1_regimens.notes: Carboplatin/Paclitaxel + Atezolizumab was recently added as a first-line treatment option based on data from the phase 2 MARBLE trial [9]. PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitor therapy is not recommended for patients with thymoma. In patients with thymic carcinoma, there is concern for a higher rate of immune-related adverse events (eg, myocarditis) than seen in most other malignancies treated with PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitor therapy. See NCCN Guidelines for the Management of Immunotherapy-Related Toxicities. Atezolizumab and hyaluronidase-tqjs subcutaneous injection may be substituted for IV atezolizumab, with different dosing and administration instructions.

trial_data.other_data: Phase II study of everolimus in patients with thymoma and thymic carcinoma previously treated with cisplatin-based chemotherapy

tier_1_regimens.notes: Everolimus is a preferred subsequent systemic therapy option for thymoma. It has demonstrated activity in patients previously treated with cisplatin-based chemotherapy [10].

trial_data.other_data: Capecitabine plus gemcitabine in thymic epithelial tumors: final analysis of a phase II trial

tier_1_regimens.notes: Gemcitabine/Capecitabine is a preferred subsequent therapy for thymoma. Gemcitabine or capecitabine may also be given as single agents. For information regarding DPYD testing, see NCCN Guidelines for Colon Cancer. Patients with certain genetic variants of DPYD that lead to DPD deficiency may be at increased risk of serious and life-threatening toxicities when treated with fluorouracil or capecitabine [11,12].

tier_1_regimens.notes: Octreotide is a preferred subsequent therapy for thymoma, but only if octreotide scan or dotatate PET/CT (dotatate PET/CT preferred if available) is positive for octreotide-avid disease. Nuclear medicine scan (octreotide scan or dotatate PET/CT) is required to assess for octreotide-avid disease [13].

tier_1_regimens.notes: Lenvatinib is a preferred subsequent therapy for thymic carcinoma. There is a high risk for side effects and frequent dose reductions may be needed. The REMORA trial demonstrated activity in metastatic or recurrent thymic carcinoma [20,200,205].

trial_data.other_data: Pembrolizumab in patients with thymic carcinoma: a single-arm, single-centre, phase 2 study

tier_1_regimens.notes: Pembrolizumab is a preferred subsequent therapy for thymic carcinoma. It is active as subsequent therapy but is also associated with a high rate of severe immune-related adverse events (15%). PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitor therapy is not recommended for patients with thymoma. In patients with thymic carcinoma, there is concern for a higher rate of immune-related adverse events (eg, myocarditis) than seen in most other malignancies treated with PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitor therapy. Grade 3–4 myocarditis has been reported in 5%–9% of patients with thymic carcinomas receiving pembrolizumab. Pembrolizumab and berahyaluronidase alfa-pmph subcutaneous injection may be substituted for IV pembrolizumab, with different dosing and administration instructions. See NCCN Guidelines for the Management of Immunotherapy-Related Toxicities [21,22,182,201].

trial_data.other_data: Partial response with sunitinib observed in 6 individuals (26%) out of 23 patients with chemotherapy-refractory thymic carcinoma

tier_1_regimens.notes: Sunitinib is a preferred subsequent therapy for thymic carcinoma, regardless of c-Kit mutation status. The single-arm phase 2 STYLE trial also evaluated sunitinib in type B3 thymoma and thymic carcinoma, with an overall response rate of 21.4% among 28 assessable patients with thymic carcinoma [23,202,203].

trial_data.other_data: Out of 32 patients, 27 had thymic carcinoma. Prior use of antiangiogenics was permitted; 4 patients previously received ramucirumab. Final median overall survival 23.4 months, 24-month overall survival rate 48.5%

PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitor therapy is not recommended in the neoadjuvant setting [24,207,208].

tier_2_handbook.definition: Thymoma and thymic carcinoma are rare neoplasms originating in the epithelial cells of the thymus gland, located in the anterior mediastinum. The thymus is a lymphoid organ situated anterior to the heart and great vessels, bordered posteriorly by the pericardium, inferiorly by the diaphragm, and laterally by the pleural sacs. Thymomas are characterized by their relatively indolent growth pattern and propensity for local invasion, though they rarely metastasize. They are the most common primary tumors of the anterior mediastinum, occurring in approximately two per million per year in the United States [3,4]. Thymic carcinomas are less common, with an estimated annual incidence of 0.48 per million in the United States [4]. These tumors are more aggressive, with frequent presentation as advanced or metastatic disease, and a markedly different histological and clinical course compared to thymomas [5-8]. A well-defined anterior mediastinal mass in the thymic bed, with negative tumor markers (AFP, beta-hCG), absence of other adenopathy, and lack of continuity with the thyroid, is highly suggestive of a thymic tumor [2]. Differentiation from other mediastinal pathologies (e.g., lymphomas, germ cell tumors, thymic cysts) is critical for management [16-22].

subtypes.name: Thymoma - Type A
subtypes.description: A thymic epithelial tumor composed of bland spindle or oval epithelial cells, rarely polygonal, with a fascicular, storiform, or pericytomatous growth pattern. Most cases lack necrosis, have a low mitotic count, and a low Ki-67 index. Atypical type A thymomas may have higher mitotic counts and focal necrosis.
subtypes.key_associations: Desirable criteria include strong expression of epithelial markers and paucity or absence of immature TdT-positive T cells throughout the tumor [THYM-D].

subtypes.name: Thymoma - Type AB
subtypes.description: A thymic tumor with a lobulated growth pattern, comprising an admixed spindle cell-predominant lymphocyte-poor component (type A) and a lymphocyte-rich component (type B). It contains bland spindle, oval, and focally polygonal thymic epithelial cells and focal or diffuse abundance of immature T cells.
subtypes.key_associations: TdT immunostaining is desirable to assess TdT-positive cell density for differential diagnosis with type A thymoma [THYM-D].

subtypes.name: Thymoma - Type B1
subtypes.description: A thymic epithelial tumor with organoid (corticomedullary) architecture showing cortical predominance. It features dispersed, non-clustered thymic epithelial cells among densely packed lymphocytes, with obligatory medullary islands.
subtypes.key_associations: Desirable criteria include cytokeratin and/or p40/p63 stains to highlight dispersed epithelial cells, and sheets of TdT-positive immature T cells interspersed with TdT-negative nodular (medullary) areas [THYM-D].

subtypes.name: Thymoma - Type B2
subtypes.description: A tumor with a lobulated architecture, abundance of lymphocytes, and polygonal/oval neoplastic epithelial cells that are more numerous than in the normal thymic cortex and often present in clusters.
subtypes.key_associations: Desirable criteria include keratin and/or p40/p63 stains to highlight the increased density of dispersed and/or clustered epithelial cells compared with the normal thymic cortex [THYM-D].

subtypes.name: Thymoma - Type B3
subtypes.description: A lobulated tumor consisting of sheets of mildly/moderately atypical, polygonal tumor cells, interspersed perivascular spaces, and a paucity of lymphocytes.
subtypes.key_associations: Desirable criteria include TdT immunostain to highlight the small population of immature T cells [THYM-D].

subtypes.name: Thymoma - Micronodular Thymoma with Lymphoid Stroma
subtypes.description: Characterized by multiple small discrete nodules composed of bland spindle or oval epithelial cells, within an abundant epithelial cell-free lymphoid stroma.
subtypes.key_associations: Desirable criteria include predominant CD20-positive B cells in the stroma [THYM-D].

subtypes.name: Thymoma - Metaplastic Thymoma
subtypes.description: A biphasic thymic tumor with anastomosing islands of polygonal epithelial cells, which may show variable nuclear atypia, against a background of bland-looking spindly cells.

subtypes.name: Thymoma - Lipofibroadenoma
subtypes.description: A benign thymic tumor resembling fibroadenoma of the breast, with a predominance of fibrous tissue over adipocytes and delicate strands of epithelial cells.

subtypes.name: Thymic Carcinoma - Squamous Cell Carcinoma, NOS
subtypes.description: An invasive squamous cell carcinoma of the thymus, often accompanied by desmoplastic to sclerohyaline stroma. It requires exclusion of invasion from adjacent pulmonary carcinoma or metastasis.
subtypes.key_associations: Desirable criteria include positive immunostaining for p63/p40, CD5, KIT (CD117), FOXN1, and/or CD205. Assay of KIT mutation may identify a potential therapeutic target [THYM-D].

subtypes.name: Thymic Carcinoma - Basaloid Carcinoma
subtypes.description: A basaloid carcinoma of the thymus with nests or cystic spaces lined by basaloid neoplastic cells with peripheral palisading.
subtypes.key_associations: Desirable criteria include immunostaining positive for p63/p40 and/or KIT (CD117) and negative for TTF1, neuroendocrine markers, and NUT [THYM-D].

subtypes.name: Thymic Carcinoma - Lymphoepithelial Carcinoma
subtypes.description: A primary thymic carcinoma featuring sheets, nests, and cords of carcinoma cells with a syncytial appearance, vesicular chromatin, and prominent nucleoli, with many admixed lymphocytes and plasma cells.
subtypes.key_associations: Desirable criteria include IHC for pancytokeratins, p63/p40. In situ hybridization for EBER does not affect diagnosis of cases with typical morphology but EBER positivity supports diagnosis in lymphocyte-poor cases [THYM-D].

subtypes.name: Thymic Carcinoma - Adenocarcinoma, NOS
subtypes.description: Adenocarcinoma of the thymus after exclusion of mediastinal metastasis from other sites and defined types of thymic adenocarcinoma.
subtypes.key_associations: Desirable criteria include immunophenotyping to rule out mediastinal metastasis or defined types of thymic adenocarcinoma [THYM-D].

subtypes.name: Thymic Carcinoma - Low-grade Papillary Adenocarcinoma
subtypes.description: A primary thymic low-grade adenocarcinoma with tubulopapillary growth, lined by cuboidal or polygonal cells.
subtypes.key_associations: Desirable criteria include immunostains to exclude papillary neoplasms of the thyroid, pleura, lung, and extrathoracic sites [THYM-D].

subtypes.name: Thymic Carcinoma - Thymic Carcinoma with Adenoid Cystic Carcinoma-like Features
subtypes.description: Thymic carcinoma morphologically similar to adenoid cystic carcinoma, but generally lacking true glands within the cribriform-basaloid islands. Requires exclusion of metastasis from salivary gland, lung, or breast.
subtypes.key_associations: Desirable criteria include IHC positive for pancytokeratins, CK5/6, p63/p40; negative staining for SMA, KIT (CD117) [THYM-D].

subtypes.name: Thymic Carcinoma - Adenocarcinoma, Enteric-type
subtypes.description: A primary thymic tumor mimicking colorectal adenocarcinoma. Requires exclusion of metastasis from an enteric primary.
subtypes.key_associations: Desirable criteria include expression of at least one marker of enteric differentiation (CK20, CDX2, MUC2) [THYM-D].

subtypes.name: Thymic Carcinoma - Adenosquamous Carcinoma
subtypes.description: A primary thymic carcinoma with both squamous and glandular differentiation, in which each component constitutes ≥10% of the tumor within a resection specimen. Requires exclusion of mucoepidermoid carcinoma.
subtypes.key_associations: Desirable criteria include the squamous component should be positive for p63/40 and/or CK5/6 [THYM-D].

subtypes.name: Thymic Carcinoma - NUT Carcinoma
subtypes.description: Demonstration of NUTM1 rearrangement by molecular methods or NUT-positive IHC in a poorly differentiated squamous cell carcinoma or other poorly differentiated carcinoma.

subtypes.name: Thymic Carcinoma - Mucoepidermoid Carcinoma
subtypes.description: A primary thymic carcinoma characterized by a combination of mucus-producing, intermediate, and squamoid cells growing in nests and cystic structures. In high-grade MEC, the diagnosis requires the presence of at least focal intracellular mucin.
subtypes.key_associations: Desirable criteria include IHC positive for pancytokeratins, CK5/6, p63/p40. MAML2 rearrangement studies might be helpful in difficult cases [THYM-D].

subtypes.name: Thymic Carcinoma - Clear Cell Carcinoma
subtypes.description: Islands and trabeculae of carcinoma cells with clear cytoplasm. Abundant hyalinized stroma in hyalinizing clear cell carcinoma.
subtypes.key_associations: Desirable criteria include cytokeratin and p40/p63 immunostaining. EWSR1 translocation for hyalinizing clear cell carcinoma [THYM-D].

subtypes.name: Thymic Carcinoma, NOS
subtypes.description: Exclusion of any of the above thymic carcinoma types. Currently comprises hepatoid carcinoma, rhabdoid carcinoma, undifferentiated large cell carcinoma with Castleman disease-like reaction, and sebaceous carcinoma.

subtypes.name: Thymic Neuroendocrine Neoplasms - Typical Carcinoid
subtypes.description: A primary thymic neuroendocrine tumor (NET) with low-grade nuclear features, neuroendocrine morphology (e.g., trabecular or rosetting), absence of necrosis, and mitotic count of <2 mitoses/2 mm².
subtypes.key_associations: Desirable criteria include strong expression of keratins and neuroendocrine markers (e.g., synaptophysin and chromogranin), usually negative for TTF1. Ki-67 can be useful in distinguishing carcinoids from high-grade LCNEC and small cell carcinoma [THYM-D].

subtypes.name: Thymic Neuroendocrine Neoplasms - Atypical Carcinoid
subtypes.description: Same as Typical Carcinoid, but with comedonecrosis and/or mitotic count of 2–10 mitoses/2 mm².
subtypes.key_associations: Same as Typical Carcinoid [THYM-D].

subtypes.name: Thymic Neuroendocrine Neoplasms - Small Cell Carcinoma
subtypes.description: Radiological evidence of thymic origin. Requires exclusion of a tumor spreading from the lung or metastatic from another extrapulmonary site to the thymus. A tumor consisting purely of small cells with characteristic morphology similar to lung SmCCs. Combined SmCC is a small carcinoma combined with another histology such as thymoma or other type of thymic carcinoma.
subtypes.key_associations: Desirable criteria include positive IHC for cytokeratins and neuroendocrine markers, as well as elevated Ki-67 (usually >50%) [THYM-D].

subtypes.name: Thymic Neuroendocrine Neoplasms - Large Cell Neuroendocrine Carcinoma
subtypes.description: Neuroendocrine morphology, mitotic count of >10 mitoses/2 mm², necrosis (often geographical), and positive neuroendocrine IHC.
subtypes.key_associations: Desirable criteria include high Ki-67 index: >30%, generally 40%–80%; negative p40 IHC [THYM-D].

tier_2_handbook.molecular_pathogenesis: The provided text does not contain specific information regarding key genomic events, driver mutations, signaling pathways, or chromosomal abnormalities with associated frequencies for thymomas or thymic carcinomas. The discussion focuses on clinical management, histologic classification, and treatment outcomes. Therefore, a detailed molecular pathogenesis section cannot be derived from this source material.

epidemiology.incidence: Thymomas are the most common primary tumors of the anterior mediastinum, with an incidence of approximately two per million per year in the United States [3,4]. Thymic carcinomas are rarer, with an estimated annual incidence of 0.48 per million in the United States [4]. They are generally rare tumors.
epidemiology.mortality: The 5-year survival rate for patients with thymomas is approximately 90% [5,12,13]. In contrast, the 5-year survival rates for patients with thymic carcinoma are closer to 60% [5,10].
epidemiology.trends: The text does not provide specific trend data on incidence or mortality over time.
epidemiology.demographics: Thymomas typically occur in adults and are rare in children and adolescents [3,22,121]. It has been reported that the incidence of thymomas is higher among certain racial and ethnic subgroups (i.e., Black and Asian individuals) [3,122]. Thymic carcinomas occur over a wide age range, including adolescents [9,11]. The epidemiology section does not provide further stratified demographic data such as sex ratios or specific racial disparities beyond the noted higher incidence in Black and Asian individuals for thymoma.

risk_factors.detail: The etiology of thymomas is unknown. Alcohol, tobacco smoking, and ionizing radiation do not appear to be risk factors for thymomas.
The incidence of thymomas is reported to be higher among Black and Asian individuals.

clinical_features.typical_presentation: Thymomas and thymic carcinomas are rare epithelial tumors originating in the thymus, with thymomas being the most common primary anterior mediastinal tumors [1,2]. Thymomas typically occur in adults and are rare in children and adolescents [3,22,121]. The etiology is unknown, with alcohol, tobacco smoking, and ionizing radiation not appearing to be risk factors [3]. There is a reported higher incidence among certain racial and ethnic subgroups, specifically Black and Asian individuals [3,122]. Patients may be asymptomatic or present with symptoms such as chest pain, cough, or dyspnea. Although thymomas can be locally invasive (e.g., to pleura, lung), they uncommonly spread to regional lymph nodes or extrathoracic sites [7,123-125]. A key characteristic is the association with autoimmune paraneoplastic syndromes, most notably myasthenia gravis, which occurs in approximately one-third of patients [126,129,130]. Hypogammaglobulinemia and pure red cell aplasia are other associated syndromes, occurring in <10% of patients [126-128,130]. Mortality is often not directly related to the thymoma itself [40].

symptoms.name: Dyspnea
symptoms.detail: A common presenting symptom, can be due to mass effect or associated myasthenia gravis.

symptoms.name: Symptoms of myasthenia gravis
symptoms.detail: Includes ptosis, diplopia, drooling, proximal muscle weakness, hoarseness, and/or dyspnea. Occurs in ~1/3 of thymoma patients [126,129,130].

symptoms.name: Symptoms from pericardial/pleural effusion
symptoms.detail: Thymic carcinomas often cause these effusions.

signs.name: Mediastinal mass on imaging
signs.detail: Well-defined round or oval mass in the thymic bed on CT, often without lymph node enlargement [33-35].

atypical_or_missed: Asymptomatic presentation with incidentally discovered mediastinal mass on imaging.
Presentation dominated by paraneoplastic syndrome (e.g., myasthenia gravis) with the thymic mass being discovered subsequently.
Thymic carcinoma presenting with advanced/metastatic disease symptoms (e.g., bone pain, neurological deficits) at diagnosis [6,9-11].
Misdiagnosis as lymphoma or germ cell tumor due to similar location but different clinical behavior and treatment [2,21,22].

red_flags: New-onset or worsening symptoms of myasthenia gravis (ptosis, diplopia, proximal weakness, dyspnea) [131-133].
Superior vena cava syndrome (facial/arm swelling, plethora, dyspnea when leaning forward).
Evidence of invasion into adjacent structures on imaging (e.g., great vessels, lung, pericardium).
Pleural or pericardial effusions causing respiratory compromise.
Symptoms suggestive of extrathoracic metastases (e.g., bone pain, neurological deficits).

diagnostic.rationale: Essential initial evaluation for a mediastinal mass to characterize location, size, and features suggestive of thymic malignancy [30-32,34]. A well-defined mass in the thymic bed, negative tumor markers, no other adenopathy, and no continuity with thyroid suggests a thymic tumor [2].
diagnostic.key_findings: Well-defined round or oval mass in the anterior mediastinum, often without lymph node enlargement [33-35].

diagnostic.rationale: Can better discriminate thymic malignancy from thymic cyst or hyperplasia compared to CT, potentially avoiding unnecessary thymectomy. Also appropriate if patients cannot tolerate iodinated contrast [35,36].
diagnostic.key_findings: Superior soft tissue contrast for evaluating invasion and differentiating masses.

diagnostic.rationale: Recommended for locally advanced or metastatic disease to obtain tissue diagnosis [THYM-2].
May be considered if core biopsy is not feasible or not diagnostic. A transpleural approach should be avoided due to risk of seeding [THYM-2, 132,138].

staging.rationale: Primary imaging for local staging and assessing resectability [THYM-1, THYM-4].
staging.indication: All patients at diagnosis and for surveillance.

staging.rationale: As clinically indicated, for comprehensive staging to evaluate extent of disease, including nodal and distant metastases [THYM-1, THYM-4].
staging.indication: Clinical suspicion of advanced or metastatic disease, or for restaging at recurrence.

staging.rationale: Appropriate alternative to CT in certain clinical situations, e.g., for better soft tissue detail or if contrast allergy exists [THYM-3, THYM-4, footnote m].
staging.indication: When CT is inconclusive or contraindicated.

staging.rationale: Consider in thymic carcinoma, which can metastasize to brain [Discussion].
staging.indication: Advanced or recurrent thymic carcinoma, or neurological symptoms.

biomarkers.rationale: To rule out germ cell tumors, which can also present as anterior mediastinal masses [THYM-1, 37].
biomarkers.when: Initial evaluation of any anterior mediastinal mass.
biomarkers.mandatory: Yes, as part of initial workup.

biomarkers.rationale: To assess for somatostatin receptor expression, which informs use of octreotide therapy in thymoma [THYM-C 2 of 3, 13,14]. Dotatate PET/CT is preferred if available.
biomarkers.when: For recurrent/refractory thymoma being considered for octreotide therapy.
biomarkers.mandatory: No, but mandatory to determine eligibility for octreotide.

biomarkers.rationale: Essential for subtyping thymic epithelial tumors per WHO classification (e.g., cytokeratins, p40/p63, TdT, CD5, KIT, CD117) [THYM-D, 1,2].
biomarkers.when: On all resected or biopsied specimens.

biomarkers.test: Molecular testing (e.g., NGS, FISH for NUTM1)
biomarkers.rationale: Consider for certain subtypes (e.g., NUT carcinoma requires demonstration of NUTM1 rearrangement) or to identify potential therapeutic targets (e.g., KIT mutation) [THYM-D, 1,2].
biomarkers.when: For specific histologic subtypes or to guide therapy in advanced disease.

do_not_miss: Complete neurological evaluation for signs of myasthenia gravis or other paraneoplastic syndromes in all thymoma patients [THYM-1, a].
Assessment for distress as per NCCN Distress Thermometer [THYM-1, c].
Evaluation of resectability by an experienced thoracic surgeon in a multidisciplinary setting [THYM-2, e].
avoid: Transpleural biopsy approach for suspected thymoma due to high risk of seeding and converting stage I to IV disease [THYM-A, 132,138].
Surgical biopsy if resectable thymoma is strongly suspected based on clinical and radiologic features, due to potential for tumor seeding [THYM-A].
PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitor therapy for thymoma due to high risk of immune-related adverse events [THYM-C 1 of 3, d].

staging.system: Both the Masaoka-Koga staging system and the AJCC 8th/9th edition TNM system are provided. Treatment recommendations in the NCCN Guidelines primarily refer to Masaoka-Koga staging, as much clinical evidence was generated using this system [Discussion]. The AJCC 9th edition TNM definitions are provided in Table 2 and the prognostic groups in Table 3 [ST-2, ST-3].

t_stages.description: Tumor limited to the thymus with or without encapsulation, or directly invades into the mediastinal fat only, or directly invades the mediastinal pleura but does not involve any other mediastinal structure.
Tumor ≤5 cm in greatest dimension with characteristics of T1.
Tumor >5 cm in greatest dimension with characteristics of T1.
Tumor with direct invasion of the pericardium (either partial or full thickness), or the lung, or the phrenic nerve.
Tumor with direct invasion into any of the following: brachiocephalic vein, superior vena cava, chest wall, or extrapericardial pulmonary arteries or veins.
Tumor with direct invasion into any of the following: aorta (ascending, arch, or descending), arch vessels, intrapericardial pulmonary artery or veins, myocardium, trachea, esophagus.

n_stages.description: Regional lymph nodes cannot be assessed.
No tumor involvement of regional lymph node(s).
Tumor involvement of anterior (perithymic) lymph nodes.
Tumor involvement of deep intrathoracic or cervical lymph nodes (e.g., paratracheal, subcarinal, aortopulmonary window, hilar, jugular, and/or supraclavicular node).

m_stages.description: Pulmonary intraparenchymal nodule or other distant metastasis.
Microscopic confirmation of separate pleural or pericardial nodule(s).
Microscopic confirmation of pulmonary intraparenchymal nodule or other distant metastasis.

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Early-stage disease, excellent prognosis with surgery alone.
stage_groups.five_yr_survival: Approximately 90% for thymoma [137]; 5-year survival for thymic carcinoma with R0 resection is ~60-70% [9,10].

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Local invasion of pericardium, lung, or phrenic nerve.
Invasion into major vessels (brachiocephalic vein, SVC), chest wall, or extrapericardial pulmonary vessels.
Invasion into aorta, arch vessels, intrapericardial pulmonary vessels, heart, trachea, or esophagus.

stage_groups.criteria: Any T, N1, M0 OR Any T, N0-N1, M1a
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Involvement of anterior lymph nodes (N1) OR pleural/pericardial dissemination (M1a).
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Often palliative, but multimodal therapy with curative intent may be attempted in selected cases.

stage_groups.criteria: Any T, N2, M0-M1a OR Any T, Any N, M1b
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Involvement of deep intrathoracic/cervical nodes (N2) OR distant metastasis (M1b).

staging_pearls: The Masaoka-Koga system is more commonly used in clinical practice and for treatment decisions in these guidelines [Discussion].
Resectability, defined as complete (R0) resection, is a critical prognostic factor and should be determined by a thoracic surgeon in a multidisciplinary context [THYM-2, e].
For thymoma, late recurrences (≥10 years) can occur due to indolent growth, necessitating long-term surveillance [Discussion].
Thymic carcinomas are often diagnosed at advanced stages (III or IV) and have a higher risk of recurrence compared to thymomas [9,10, Discussion].
The AJCC 9th edition TNM system provides more granular T staging (T1a vs T1b) and formalizes nodal staging (N1, N2), which can be useful for prognostication [ST-2, ST-3].
Pleural or pericardial nodules are classified as M1a (Stage IVA), while parenchymal pulmonary nodules or other distant metastases are M1b (Stage IVB) [ST-2].

management_principles.overview: Thymomas and thymic carcinomas are rare thymic epithelial tumors. Management is complex and requires a multimodal approach involving surgery, radiation therapy (RT), and/or systemic therapy, depending on the disease stage and completeness of resection. A complete (R0) surgical resection is a critical prognostic factor and the primary oncologic goal for resectable tumors [5,7,12,52-60]. Treatment philosophy emphasizes individualization based on disease extent, histologic subtype (WHO classification), and resectability, which is defined as the potential for an R0 resection. All patients should be evaluated and managed by a multidisciplinary team with experience in these tumors [5,7,13,22,134].

by_intent.patient_group: Patients with surgically resectable disease (Masaoka-Koga stage I–III) or localized advanced disease amenable to multimodal therapy.
by_intent.approach: The primary treatment is surgical resection (total thymectomy and excision of contiguous disease). For stage I with no capsular invasion (R0), observation is recommended postoperatively. For higher stages (II–IV) or with capsular invasion, postoperative RT is recommended even after R0 resection. For R1 or R2 resections, postoperative RT with or without chemotherapy is recommended. For locally advanced, potentially resectable disease (uncertain R0 resection), neoadjuvant systemic therapy should be considered to improve resectability before surgery [5,7,12,22,61-64,135,136].

by_intent.patient_group: Patients with unresectable advanced, metastatic, or recurrent disease.
by_intent.approach: Treatment is directed at symptom control, disease stabilization, and prolonging survival. Options include definitive RT (conventional fractionation or SABR for limited lesions), concurrent chemoradiation, systemic therapy, or observation, depending on disease dissemination and patient fitness. Local therapies like image-guided thermal ablation may be considered for select metastatic or recurrent lesions. The natural history can be indolent, even for metastatic thymoma, allowing for durable local control with RT [5,13,56,98,100,114-117,153-155].

management_principles.mdt_requirements: All patients with a likely thymic tumor should be treated by a multidisciplinary team (MDT) with experience in the management of thymomas and thymic carcinomas. The team should include radiation oncologists, thoracic surgeons, medical oncologists, neurologists (especially for patients with myasthenia gravis), pathologists, and diagnostic imaging specialists [5,7,13,22,134]. Determination of resectability must be made by a thoracic surgeon, preferably with a primary focus on thoracic oncology, and in multidisciplinary consultation as needed [e].
management_principles.performance_status_guide: The guidelines do not specify explicit performance status (PS) thresholds for treatment eligibility. However, the decision for surgery, definitive RT, or systemic therapy is inherently based on a patient's overall medical fitness and ability to tolerate the proposed treatment. Patients must be medically controlled prior to surgical resection, especially for paraneoplastic syndromes like myasthenia gravis. The MDT discussion integrates PS and comorbidities to determine the most appropriate, individualized treatment plan [a, THYM-2].

nodes.criteria: Thymoma, clinically resectable, Masaoka-Koga stage I (no capsular invasion).

options.label: Surgical resection (total thymectomy and complete tumor excision)
options.recommendation: Primary treatment. Avoid surgical biopsy if thymoma is strongly suspected [a]. Avoid transpleural biopsy approach [a].

options.label: Observation
options.recommendation: For patients with completely resected (R0) stage I thymoma and no capsular invasion, no adjuvant therapy is recommended due to low recurrence risk and absence of survival benefit [22,91,93-96].

adjuvant_logic.summary: No adjuvant RT or chemotherapy recommended for R0 resection, Masaoka-Koga stage I.

nodes.criteria: Thymoma, surgically resectable. Masaoka-Koga stage II–IV with capsular invasion, OR R0 resection with high-risk features, OR R1 (microscopic residual) resection.

options.label: Surgical resection
options.recommendation: Primary treatment with goal of R0 resection [THYM-2].

options.label: Adjuvant postoperative RT
options.recommendation: Should be considered after R0 resection if capsular invasion present or for stage II–IV. Recommended for R1 resection. Dose: 45–50 Gy for clear/close margins; 54 Gy for microscopically positive margins [i]. Decisions about adjuvant RT should be based on MDT evaluation [j].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant RT is recommended for stage II–IV disease after R0 resection, or for any R1 resection, regardless of stage.

nodes.criteria: Thymoma, surgically resectable, but R0 resection deemed uncertain.

options.label: Neoadjuvant systemic therapy
options.recommendation: Preoperative systemic therapy should be considered to improve resectability and increase odds of R0 resection [f,61-64]. Followed by reassessment for surgery.

options.label: Definitive RT ± chemotherapy
options.recommendation: Total dose of 60–66 Gy (1.8–2.0 Gy/fraction) is recommended for gross residual disease [3-6].

options.label: Systemic therapy ± RT
options.recommendation: Postoperative chemotherapy in combination with RT is an option [k,l]. There is diversity of opinion on optimal approach [l].

adjuvant_logic.summary: For R2 resection, definitive RT (with or without chemotherapy) or systemic therapy combined with RT is recommended.

management_pathways.title: Management of Advanced, Metastatic, or Recurrent Thymoma
branching_basis: Resectability of primary and metastatic sites

nodes.criteria: Thymoma. Locally advanced, solitary, or ipsilateral pleural metastasis. Potentially resectable disease (primary and isolated metastases deemed resectable).

options.label: Neoadjuvant systemic therapy
options.recommendation: To reduce tumor size and increase odds of complete resection [61-64,150].

options.label: Surgical resection of primary tumor and isolated metastases
options.recommendation: Goal is complete resection of all disease [g].

options.label: Postoperative RT
options.recommendation: Consider postoperative RT after resection of primary and metastases [i].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Aim for R0 resection with possible neoadjuvant therapy. Adjuvant RT may be considered postoperatively.

nodes.criteria: Thymoma. Locally advanced disease, deemed unresectable.

options.label: Definitive RT
options.recommendation: Dose 60–66 Gy for unresectable disease [3].

options.label: Concurrent chemoradiation
options.recommendation: Carboplatin/paclitaxel or cisplatin/etoposide can be used with RT as definitive concurrent chemoradiation [b,k].

options.label: Systemic therapy alone
options.recommendation: For symptom control and disease control.

options.label: Systemic therapy
options.recommendation: To alleviate symptoms and achieve disease control [k,156-158].

options.label: Surgery
options.recommendation: May be considered for locoregional recurrence if clinically feasible, similar to approach for newly diagnosed disease [13,56,160-163].

options.label: RT
options.recommendation: For locoregional recurrence or limited metastases (SABR for focal lesions, conventional fractionation for larger lesions) [i,114,115,155].

options.label: Systemic therapy
options.recommendation: For extrathoracic recurrence or as part of multimodal approach [k].

options.label: Other local therapies
options.recommendation: Image-guided thermal ablation may be considered for select cases [o].

management_pathways.title: Management of Surgically Resectable Thymic Carcinoma

nodes.criteria: Thymic carcinoma, clinically resectable. Masaoka-Koga stage I (no capsular invasion).

options.label: Surgical resection
options.recommendation: Primary treatment. Goal is R0 resection [THYM-2].

options.label: Observation
options.recommendation: No postoperative therapy recommended for completely resected (R0) stage I disease with no capsular invasion [193].

adjuvant_logic.summary: No adjuvant therapy for R0 resection, Masaoka-Koga stage I.

nodes.criteria: Thymic carcinoma, surgically resectable. Masaoka-Koga stage II–IV, OR any positive margin (R1 or R2).

options.label: Surgical resection
options.recommendation: Primary treatment with goal of R0 resection [THYM-2].

options.label: Postoperative RT
options.recommendation: Can be considered for stage II–IV after R0 resection. Recommended for R1 or R2 resection, regardless of stage. Dose: 45–50 Gy for clear/close margins; 54 Gy for microscopically positive margins; 60–66 Gy for gross residual disease (R2) [i,3-6].

options.label: Postoperative systemic therapy ± RT
options.recommendation: Postoperative chemotherapy (in combination with RT) is recommended as an option for incomplete resection (R1/R2) [k,l]. There is a diversity of opinion on treatment approach [l].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant RT is recommended for R1/R2 resections and may be considered for R0 resections in stage II–IV.

nodes.criteria: Thymic carcinoma, surgically resectable, but R0 resection deemed uncertain.

options.label: Neoadjuvant systemic therapy
options.recommendation: Preoperative systemic therapy to reduce tumor size and increase odds of R0 resection [f,61-64,150,151].

management_pathways.title: Management of Advanced, Metastatic, or Recurrent Thymic Carcinoma

nodes.criteria: Thymic carcinoma. Potentially resectable disease (primary and isolated metastases).

options.label: Neoadjuvant systemic therapy
options.recommendation: To increase odds of complete resection [150,151].

options.label: Surgical resection of primary and isolated metastases
options.recommendation: Followed by postoperative RT and/or systemic therapy [g,150,151,153].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Aim for R0 resection with possible neoadjuvant therapy. Adjuvant RT and/or systemic therapy is typically recommended postoperatively.

options.label: Definitive RT
options.recommendation: Dose 60–66 Gy for unresectable disease [3,100].

options.label: Concurrent chemoradiation
options.recommendation: Carboplatin/paclitaxel or cisplatin/etoposide can be used with RT [b,k,100].

options.label: Systemic therapy
options.recommendation: For systemic disease control.

options.label: Local therapy for metastases
options.recommendation: SABR may be appropriate for limited focal metastases; conventional fractionation for larger metastases [114,115]. Image-guided thermal ablation for select cases [o].

nodes.criteria: Thymic carcinoma. Evidence of extrathoracic metastases.

options.label: Systemic therapy
options.recommendation: For symptom control and disease control [k].

options.label: Surgery
options.recommendation: May be considered for isolated recurrence if R0 resection is achievable, similar to newly diagnosed disease [9,10,191,192].

options.label: RT
options.recommendation: For locoregional recurrence or limited metastases [i,114,115,155].

options.label: Subsequent systemic therapy
options.recommendation: For systemic recurrence or progression after prior therapy. See subsequent systemic therapy options for thymic carcinoma [k].

options.label: Other local therapies
options.recommendation: Image-guided thermal ablation may be considered [o].

pretreatment_evaluation.category: Initial Diagnostic Workup for Mediastinal Mass

items.recommendation: Mandatory initial imaging to characterize the mediastinal mass [THYM-1].

items.text: Chest MRI with and without contrast
items.recommendation: As clinically indicated. MRI is often better than CT for discriminating thymic malignancy versus cyst/hyperplasia, potentially avoiding unnecessary thymectomy [b,36].
If appropriate, to rule out germ cell tumors [THYM-1,37].
Refer to NCCN Distress Thermometer and Problem List [c,THYM-1].

items.text: Clinical evaluation for signs of myasthenia gravis and other paraneoplastic syndromes
items.recommendation: Patients with thymoma should be evaluated clinically [a,THYM-1].

items.text: Core needle biopsy of the mediastinal mass
items.recommendation: Recommended for locally advanced or metastatic disease to obtain tissue diagnosis [g,THYM-2].
May be considered if core biopsy is not feasible or not diagnostic. Avoid transpleural approach due to risk of tumor seeding [a, g, THYM-2].

items.text: Avoid surgical biopsy if resectable thymoma is strongly suspected
items.recommendation: Due to substantial potential of tumor seeding when capsule is violated [THYM-A].

items.text: Assessment per WHO Histologic Classification and Cancer Protocol
items.recommendation: Use WHO classification to distinguish thymoma subtypes (A, AB, B1, B2, B3) from thymic carcinomas. College of American Pathologists cancer protocol may be useful [1,2,70, THYM-D].
Patients should be evaluated and medically controlled prior to surgery [a, THYM-A].
Essential for all patients, especially for determining resectability and planning multimodal therapy [THYM-2].
Traditionally used for treatment decisions. Provided in guidelines [ST-1].
Provided in guidelines [ST-2, ST-3]. Treatment recommendations often refer to Masaoka-Koga staging due to historical evidence base.

surgery.role: Surgery is the primary and potentially curative treatment modality for resectable thymomas and thymic carcinomas. Complete (R0) resection is a critical prognostic factor [5,7,12,52-60].
principles: Should be performed by thoracic surgeons with experience in managing thymomas and thymic carcinomas [THYM-A].
Locally advanced and resectable stage ≥ II cases should be discussed by an MDT [THYM-A].
Surgical biopsy should be avoided if resectable thymoma is strongly suspected to prevent tumor seeding [THYM-A].
Biopsy of a possible thymoma should avoid a transpleural approach to prevent converting stage I to stage IV [a, THYM-A].
Goal is complete excision of the lesion with total thymectomy and complete resection of contiguous and noncontiguous disease [THYM-2, THYM-A].

Complete resection may require resection of adjacent structures (pericardium, phrenic nerve, pleura, lung, major vascular structures). Bilateral phrenic nerve resection should be avoided due to severe respiratory morbidity [THYM-A].
Surgical clips should be placed at areas of close margins, residual disease, or tumor adhesion to guide RT [THYM-A].
During thymectomy, pleural surfaces should be examined for metastases. Resection of pleural metastases to achieve complete gross resection is appropriate if feasible [THYM-A].

Minimally invasive procedures (VATS, RATS) for thymectomy may be considered if all oncologic principles of open thymectomy are met and performed in specialized centers by experienced surgeons [1-15, THYM-A].

procedures.name: Total Thymectomy (Open or Minimally Invasive)
procedures.approach: Transsternal (open), Video-assisted thoracoscopic surgery (VATS), or Robotic-assisted thoracoscopic surgery (RATS).
procedures.indications: Standard of care for resectable thymomas and thymic carcinomas [THYM-2].
procedures.key_complications: Phrenic nerve injury (especially bilateral), respiratory morbidity, myasthenia crisis (if MG present), infection, bleeding.

surgery.resectability_criteria: Resectability is defined as the potential for complete (R0) resection. Determination should be made by a thoracic surgeon, with primary focus on thoracic oncology, and in multidisciplinary consultation as needed [e, THYM-2, THYM-4].
surgery.pre_operative_optimisation: Patients with thymoma should be medically controlled for myasthenia gravis and other paraneoplastic syndromes prior to surgery. If R0 resection is considered uncertain, preoperative systemic therapy should be considered [a, f, THYM-A, THYM-2].

radiation_therapy.role: RT is used in multiple settings: as definitive therapy for unresectable disease, as adjuvant therapy after surgery (especially for incomplete resection or higher-stage disease), and as local therapy for recurrent or metastatic lesions [90].
principles: Recommendations should be made by radiation oncologists with experience in managing thymomas and thymic carcinomas [THYM-B].
Definitive RT for unresectable disease, incompletely resected invasive thymoma or thymic carcinoma, or as adjuvant therapy after systemic therapy and surgery [THYM-B].
Close communication between radiation oncologist and surgeon regarding operative findings is essential to define the target volume at risk [THYM-B].
Communication with pathologist regarding histology, disease extent, and surgical margins is important [THYM-B].
Review of preoperative imaging and co-registration into the planning system are helpful [THYM-B].
Extensive elective nodal irradiation (ENI) is not recommended because thymomas do not commonly metastasize to regional lymph nodes [7, THYM-B].

dose_frameworks.name: Adjuvant RT, clear/close margins
dose_frameworks.indication: Postoperative adjuvant treatment for clear or close surgical margins [THYM-B].

dose_frameworks.name: Adjuvant RT, microscopically positive margins (R1)
dose_frameworks.indication: Postoperative adjuvant treatment for microscopically positive (R1) resection margins [THYM-B].

dose_frameworks.name: Definitive RT for unresectable disease or gross residual disease (R2)
dose_frameworks.indication: For patients with unresectable disease or gross residual disease (R2) after surgery [3, THYM-B].

dose_frameworks.name: Palliative RT
dose_frameworks.total_dose: Various (e.g., 8 Gy in 1 fx, 20 Gy in 5 fx, 30 Gy in 10 fx up to definitive doses)
dose_frameworks.indication: Palliative setting; given the long natural history, even definitive doses may be considered for durable local control [THYM-B].

approaches.name: 3D Conformal Radiation Therapy (3D-CRT)
approaches.concurrent_chemotherapy: As per clinical setting (e.g., cisplatin/etoposide, carboplatin/paclitaxel)
approaches.toxicities: Standard RT toxicities; conservative heart dose limits recommended [THYM-B].

approaches.name: Intensity-Modulated Radiation Therapy (IMRT) / Volumetric Modulated Arc Therapy (VMAT)
approaches.indication: Preferred over 3D-CRT to improve target coverage and dose conformity and further reduce cardiac dose [THYM-B].
approaches.toxicities: May reduce cardiac dose compared to 3D-CRT.

approaches.name: Proton Therapy
approaches.indication: Appropriate and shown to improve dosimetry, allowing for better sparing of normal organs (lungs, heart) with favorable local control and toxicity [8,9, THYM-B].

approaches.name: Stereotactic Ablative Radiotherapy (SABR/SBRT)
approaches.dose_fractionation: High dose per fraction (e.g., 50 Gy in 5 fractions)
approaches.indication: May be appropriate for limited focal metastases [114,115, THYM-B].
approaches.toxicities: High local control rates with acceptable toxicity for small metastases.

systemic_therapy.overview: Systemic therapy is used in multiple settings: neoadjuvant for potentially resectable advanced disease, definitive for unresectable advanced/metastatic disease, and adjuvant for incompletely resected disease. Treatment regimens differ for thymoma and thymic carcinoma. Thymomas are considered chemosensitive, while thymic carcinomas are generally less chemosensitive. PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitor therapy is not recommended for thymoma and is associated with high rates of immune-related adverse events in thymic carcinoma [d, e, 180-182, 201, THYM-C].

by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Based on high response rates in clinical trials and data from the RYTHMIC database [158, 165].
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: CAP/Prednisone [4], ADOC [5], Cisplatin/Etoposide (PE) [6], Cisplatin/Etoposide/Ifosfamide [7], Carboplatin/Paclitaxel [2,3]. Carboplatin/Paclitaxel and PE can be used with RT as definitive concurrent chemoradiation [b].

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Everolimus [10], Gemcitabine/Capecitabine [11,12], Octreotide (if octreotide scan or dotatate PET/CT positive) ± Prednisone [13,14], Pemetrexed [15], [THYM-C].
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Active agents in pretreated patients based on phase II trials.
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Fluorouracil/Leucovorin [16], Paclitaxel [17]. Etoposide [18] and Ifosfamide [19] may be useful in certain circumstances [THYM-C].
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: Octreotide receptor positivity (via octreotide scan or dotatate PET/CT) for octreotide-based regimens [i, THYM-C]. DPYD testing for fluoropyrimidine therapy [g].

by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Carboplatin/Paclitaxel is based on phase II trials showing activity [2,3]. Addition of ramucirumab is based on the phase 2 RELEVENT trial showing high ORR [8,197].
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: CAP [1], CAP/Prednisone [4], ADOC [5], PE [6], Cisplatin/Etoposide/Ifosfamide [7], Carboplatin/Paclitaxel + Atezolizumab [9], [THYM-C]. Carboplatin/Paclitaxel and PE can be used with RT [b].
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: None specified for chemotherapy. Atezolizumab not recommended in neoadjuvant setting [e].

by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Gemcitabine/Capecitabine [11,12], lenvatinib (REMORA trial, ORR 38%) [20,205], pembrolizumab (ORR 22.5%) [21], and sunitinib (ORR 26% in refractory disease) [23,203] have demonstrated activity in phase II trials.
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Axitinib + Avelumab [24], Everolimus [10], Fluorouracil/Leucovorin [16], Paclitaxel [17], Pemetrexed [15]. Etoposide [18] and Ifosfamide [19] may be useful in certain circumstances [THYM-C].
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitor therapy (pembrolizumab) not recommended for thymoma and associated with high immune-related adverse events (including myocarditis) in thymic carcinoma [d, THYM-C]. Lenvatinib has high side effect risk and may require dose reductions [j, THYM-C]. DPYD testing for fluoropyrimidine therapy [g].

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitor therapy is NOT recommended for patients with thymoma [d, THYM-C, 180-182].
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Concern for severe immune-related adverse events (e.g., myocarditis) in a population predisposed to paraneoplastic autoimmune disorders.

key_regimens.name: Carboplatin/Paclitaxel
key_regimens.setting: Thymic Carcinoma - First-Line (also listed for Thymoma)

key_regimens.name: Carboplatin/Paclitaxel + Ramucirumab
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Not specified; standard chemotherapy and antiangiogenic toxicities. Patients with untreated brain metastases or major contraindications to antiangiogenics were excluded from the trial [c].

key_regimens.name: Carboplatin/Paclitaxel + Atezolizumab
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Not specified; standard chemotherapy and immunotherapy toxicities. Atezolizumab not recommended in neoadjuvant setting [e].

key_regimens.name: Gemcitabine/Capecitabine
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Not specified; standard chemotherapy toxicities. Gemcitabine or capecitabine may be given as single agents [h].

treatment_response_assessment.title: Post-Treatment Evaluation and Surveillance
treatment_response_assessment.timing: Surveillance imaging should be performed routinely after completion of primary treatment.
treatment_response_assessment.response_logic: Imaging response is assessed using CT with contrast. For thymoma, the ITMIG has proposed radiographic assessment using modified RECIST criteria [123], but the guidelines do not mandate a specific response assessment method.

imaging_recommendations.scenario: After treatment for completely resected Masaoka-Koga stage I thymoma (no capsular invasion).
imaging_recommendations.recommendation: Chest CT with contrast every 6–12 months for 2 years, then annually until year 10. MRI is an alternative in certain clinical situations [m, THYM-3].

imaging_recommendations.scenario: After treatment for all other stages of thymoma.
imaging_recommendations.recommendation: Chest CT with contrast every 6 months for 2 years, then annually until year 10 [m, THYM-3].

imaging_recommendations.scenario: After treatment for completely resected Masaoka-Koga stage I thymic carcinoma (no capsular invasion).
imaging_recommendations.recommendation: Chest CT with contrast every 6–12 months for 2 years, then annually until year 5 [m, THYM-3].

imaging_recommendations.scenario: After treatment for all other stages of thymic carcinoma.
imaging_recommendations.recommendation: Chest CT with contrast every 3–6 months for 2 years, then annually until year 5 [m, THYM-3].

imaging_recommendations.scenario: During active treatment for advanced/recurrent disease.
imaging_recommendations.recommendation: Chest CT with contrast and FDG-PET/CT (skull base to mid-thigh) as clinically indicated [p, THYM-4].

treatment_response_assessment.biopsy_or_salvage_logic: The duration for surveillance has not been established [n]. Patients with thymoma have an increased risk for second malignancies, although no particular screening studies are recommended. Recurrent disease should be managed by the MDT, with treatment options similar to those for newly diagnosed disease (see Management Pathways) [THYM-3, THYM-4]. Late recurrences (≥10 years) are documented for thymoma [56].

prognosis.overall: Thymomas and thymic carcinomas are rare thymic epithelial tumors originating in the thymus. Among generally rare tumors of the anterior mediastinum, thymomas are the most common primary tumors, occurring in approximately two per million per year in the United States [4]. Thymic carcinomas are even less common, with an estimated annual incidence of 0.48 per million in the United States [4]. Thymomas are less likely to be invasive and metastasize compared to thymic carcinomas [5-8]. The 5-year survival rate of patients with thymomas is approximately 90% [5,12,13], while the 5-year survival rates for patients with thymic carcinoma are closer to 60% [5,10]. It has been reported that the incidence of thymomas is higher among certain racial and ethnic subgroups, including Black and Asian individuals [3,122]. Patients with thymic carcinomas often present with advanced or metastatic disease at diagnosis [6,9-11]. Many patients with thymic carcinoma may have advanced or metastatic disease at presentation; in two separate studies, over three-quarters of patients with thymic carcinoma were diagnosed with Masaoka stage III or IV disease [9,10].

by_stage.context: 10-year survival rate approximately 90% for patients with completely resected Masaoka stage I thymoma [137]. Patients with completely resected stage I thymoma and no capsular invasion are at low recurrence risk and do not require adjuvant therapy [22,91,93,95,96].

10-year survival rate approximately 70% for completely resected stage II thymoma [137]. There are conflicting data on whether patients with stage II thymoma with complete resection will derive benefit from postoperative radiation [91,93,95,96,141].

10-year survival rate approximately 55% for completely resected stage III thymoma [137]. Higher stage thymomas have a greater risk of disease recurrence and therefore postoperative therapy may be warranted even after complete resection [91,92,95,142,143].

10-year survival rate approximately 35% for completely resected stage IVa thymoma [137]. The National Cancer Data Base study found that postoperative RT was associated with longer survival, particularly among those with Masaoka-Koga stage IIB and III disease [91].

10-year survival rate of patients with thymoma and complete resection was significantly higher than those with incomplete resection or biopsy (76% vs 28%) [56]. Complete resection is considered an important prognostic factor [12,56-60].

The 5-year survival rate of patients who have an R0 resection for thymic carcinoma is estimated to be around 60% to 70% [9,10]. Among patients with thymic carcinoma, the 5-year survival rate of those with complete resection (66.9%) was higher than those with subtotal resection (30.1%) or inoperable disease (24.2%) [5].

The 5-year survival rates for thymic carcinoma are closer to 60% overall [5,10]. In a retrospective analysis of 1042 patients, the cumulative incidence of recurrence at 5 years was 35%, while the 10-year cumulative incidence of recurrence was 40% [10].

prognostic_factors: Completeness of resection (R0 vs R1 vs R2) - R0 resection is an important prognostic factor; 10-year survival 76% with complete resection vs 28% with incomplete resection or biopsy [12,56-60]
Masaoka-Koga stage - Higher stages have greater risk of recurrence; survival decreases with advancing stage [5,7,39-43]
Histologic subtype - Thymic carcinoma carries worse prognosis than thymoma [5,10]
Age - Thymic carcinomas occur over a wide age range including adolescents, while thymomas typically occur in adults [9,11,121]
Paraneoplastic syndromes - Presence may impact surgical management timing [126-130]
prognosis.prognostic_context: Mortality in many thymoma patients is not related to the thymoma itself [40]. The 10-year survival rate of patients with thymoma and complete resection was significantly higher than those with incomplete resection or biopsy (76% vs 28%) [56]. For completely resected thymic carcinoma, the 5-year survival rate ranges from 60% to 70% [9,10]. The presence of myasthenia gravis or other paraneoplastic syndromes does not appear to adversely affect survival but requires careful preoperative management [126-130]. Recurrence rates vary widely in studies, with some late recurrences occurring ≥10 years after surgical resection documented for thymoma [56].

disease_related.management: Approximately one-third of patients with thymomas may have myasthenia gravis, the most common paraneoplastic syndrome associated with thymoma [126,129,130]. Patients should be evaluated clinically for signs of myasthenia gravis before surgical resection [131-133]. If patients have myasthenia gravis, they should receive treatment by a neurologist with experience in myasthenia gravis prior to surgical resection. Symptoms include ptosis, diplopia, drooling, proximal muscle weakness, hoarseness, and/or dyspnea [126-128].
Associated with thymoma; should be evaluated as part of paraneoplastic workup [126-128]. The clinical spectrum includes Good syndrome (thymoma-associated immunodeficiency) and beyond [127].
Associated with thymoma; estimated to occur in <10% of patients with thymoma [130]. Requires appropriate hematologic workup and management.

Other paraneoplastic syndromes have been estimated to occur in <10% of patients with thymoma [130]. All patients with thymoma should be evaluated clinically for signs of autoimmune paraneoplastic disorders [THYM-1].
Thymic carcinomas often cause pericardial and pleural effusions [9,10]. May require drainage procedures and management as clinically indicated.
Symptom that patients with thymic carcinoma may experience related to mass effect [11]. Requires urgent management and may indicate advanced disease.

Thymomas can be locally invasive to pleura and lung but uncommonly spread to regional lymph nodes or extrathoracic sites [7,123-125]. Complete resection may require resection of adjacent structures including pericardium, phrenic nerve, pleura, lung, and major vascular structures [THYM-A].

chemotherapy: PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitor therapy is not recommended for patients with thymoma due to concerns about immune-related adverse events in a population predisposed to paraneoplastic autoimmune disorders [180,181]. Among thymoma patients receiving pembrolizumab in a phase 2 trial, 71% (5/7) experienced grade ≥3 immune-related adverse events, including myocarditis [182].

In patients with thymic carcinoma, there is concern for a higher rate of immune-related adverse events (eg, myocarditis) than seen in most other malignancies treated with PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitor therapy. Grade 3–4 myocarditis has been reported in 5%–9% of patients receiving pembrolizumab [footnote d, THYM-C 1 of 3].
PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitor therapy is not recommended in the neoadjuvant setting [footnote e, THYM-C 1 of 3].
Lenvatinib has a high risk for side effects and frequent dose reductions may be needed [footnote j, THYM-C 2 of 3].
For information regarding DPYD testing related to fluorouracil and capecitabine toxicity risk, see NCCN Guidelines for Colon Cancer [179, footnote g, THYM-C 2 of 3].
immunotherapy: PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitor therapy not recommended for thymoma due to high rate of immune-related adverse events (71% grade ≥3 including myocarditis) [180-182].
For thymic carcinoma, pembrolizumab is active (response rate 22.5%) but associated with a high rate of severe immune-related adverse events (15%) [201,204].
Grade 3-4 myocarditis has been reported in 5% to 9% of thymic carcinoma patients receiving pembrolizumab, higher than rates in other malignancies [182,201].
Atezolizumab and hyaluronidase-tqjs subcutaneous injection may be substituted for IV atezolizumab with different dosing/administration [footnote f, THYM-C 1 of 3].
Pembrolizumab and berahyaluronidase alfa-pmph subcutaneous injection may be substituted for IV pembrolizumab with different dosing/administration [footnote k, THYM-C 2 of 3].
targeted_therapy: Lenvatinib: There is a high risk for side effects and frequent dose reductions may be needed [200, footnote j].
Sunitinib: Activity demonstrated regardless of c-Kit mutation status [202,203].
Everolimus: Can be used for both thymoma and thymic carcinoma in subsequent settings [171].

Ramucirumab: No published experience as preoperative therapy; patients with untreated brain metastases or major contraindications to antiangiogenics were excluded from studies [footnote c, THYM-C 1 of 3].
surgery: Bilateral phrenic nerve resection should be avoided due to severe respiratory morbidity [THYM-A].
Surgical biopsy should be avoided if resectable thymoma is strongly suspected because of substantial potential for tumor seeding when tumor capsule is violated [THYM-A].
Biopsy of possible thymoma should avoid transpleural approach due to substantial risk of converting stage I thymoma to stage IV thymoma by spreading tumor within pleural space [THYM-A, 132,138].
Surgical clips should be placed at time of resection to areas of close margins, residual disease, or tumor adhesion to unresected normal structures to help guide accurate RT when indicated [THYM-A].
radiation: Long-term cardiac sequelae from RT are a concern as these patients are younger and mostly long-term survivors. Dose to heart should be as low as reasonably achievable to reduce possibility of long-term cardiac sequelae [THYM-B 2 of 3].

More conservative limits recommended to minimize dose to heart and its substructures to maximal extent possible, as portions of pericardium typically require treatment to control disease [THYM-B 2 of 3].
Significant tumor volume motion secondary to breathing and heartbeat necessitates motion management techniques such as 4D-CT scans [THYM-B 2 of 3].

supportive_care.overview: All patients with thymoma should be evaluated clinically for signs of myasthenia gravis and other paraneoplastic syndromes with appropriate workup and treatment [THYM-1, footnote a]. Patients should be assessed for distress using the NCCN Distress Thermometer and Problem List, which includes social determinants of health [THYM-1, footnote c]. Care should be managed by a multidisciplinary team with experience in management of thymomas and thymic carcinomas [THYM-2]. The team may include radiation oncologists, thoracic surgeons, medical oncologists, neurologists, pathologists, and diagnostic imaging specialists [MS-4].
supportive_care.nutritional_support: The source document does not provide specific nutritional support recommendations for thymic malignancies. Standard supportive care for patients undergoing systemic therapy should be applied.
supportive_care.anti_emetic_protocol: The source document does not provide specific antiemetic protocol recommendations. Standard antiemetic guidelines for the chemotherapy regimens used (platinum-based combinations) should be followed.
supportive_care.gcsf_guidance: The source document does not provide specific G-CSF guidance. Supportive care should follow standard guidelines for the chemotherapy regimens employed.
supportive_care.vte_prophylaxis: The source document does not provide specific VTE prophylaxis recommendations for thymic malignancies.
supportive_care.pain_management: The source document does not provide specific pain management recommendations. Standard supportive pain management should be applied based on disease stage and treatment-related symptoms.
supportive_care.psychosocial_support: Patients should be assessed for distress. Refer to the NCCN Distress Thermometer and Problem List within the NCCN Guidelines for Distress Management (DIS-A) [THYM-1, footnote c]. Social determinants of health should be included in the distress assessment.
supportive_care.dental_care: The source document does not provide specific dental care recommendations.

follow_up.post_curative_treatment: For patients with completely resected Masaoka-Koga stage I thymoma (no capsular invasion), surveillance should include chest CT with contrast every 6 to 12 months for 2 years, then annually until year 10 [THYM-3]. For all other patients with thymoma, surveillance should include chest CT with contrast every 6 months for 2 years, then annually until year 10 [THYM-3]. For patients with completely resected Masaoka-Koga stage I thymic carcinoma (no capsular invasion), surveillance imaging should consist of chest CT with contrast every 6 to 12 months for 2 years, then annually until 5 years [THYM-3, THYM-4]. For all other patients treated for thymic carcinoma, surveillance should include chest CT every 3 to 6 months for 2 years, then annually for 5 years [THYM-3, THYM-4]. The duration, frequency, and type of imaging for surveillance have not been prospectively evaluated for either thymoma or thymic carcinoma [THYM-3, THYM-4]. The duration for surveillance has not been established [footnote n].
follow_up.surveillance_rationale: Despite excellent survival rates, surveillance remains essential for patients with thymoma as a wide range of recurrence rates has been reported [5,56,159]. Late recurrences occurring ≥10 years after surgical resection have been documented for thymoma, which may be due to the indolent growth of some thymomas [56]. For thymic carcinoma, the aggressive nature of the disease places patients at higher risk of recurrence; in one study, 51.2% of patients with thymic carcinoma developed disease recurrence compared with 7.8% of patients with thymoma [5]. A wide range of time to recurrence has been reported; one study reported time to recurrence ranged from 2 to 108 months, with a median of 11 months [9]. Progression to distant sites may occur more frequently in thymic carcinoma than thymoma [13]. Patients with thymoma have an increased risk for second malignancies, although no particular screening studies are recommended [3,4,183,184].
late_effects_screening: Cardiac toxicity monitoring - Given younger patient population and long-term survival, cardiac sequelae from RT are a concern. Heart dose should be minimized to reduce long-term cardiac effects [THYM-B 2 of 3].
Second malignancy screening - Patients with thymoma have increased risk for second malignancies; however, no particular screening studies are recommended [3,4,183,184].
Paraneoplastic syndrome monitoring - Patients with thymoma should be evaluated for signs of myasthenia gravis and other paraneoplastic syndromes at follow-up [THYM-3, footnote a].

Respiratory function monitoring - Bilateral phrenic nerve resection should be avoided to prevent severe respiratory morbidity; patients who have undergone phrenic nerve-related surgery should be monitored [THYM-A].
follow_up.recurrence_patterns: Thymomas have a propensity for limited metastatic recurrences over long periods, especially in the pleura [MS-8]. As thymoma disease progression is often locoregional (pleural or pulmonary) rather than extrathoracic, surgery or RT may be considered if clinically feasible [13,56,160-163]. For thymic carcinoma, cumulative incidence of recurrence at 5 years was 35% and at 10 years was 40% [10]. One study found that 51.2% of patients with thymic carcinoma developed disease recurrence compared with 7.8% of patients with thymoma [5]. Spread to distant sites including brain, liver, bone, and lung has been documented for thymic carcinoma [13,185].

clinical_follow_up_schedule: Thymoma, stage I (R0, no capsular invasion): Chest CT with contrast every 6-12 months for 2 years, then annually until year 10 [THYM-3]
All other thymoma patients: Chest CT with contrast every 6 months for 2 years, then annually until year 10 [THYM-3]
Thymic carcinoma, stage I (R0, no capsular invasion): Chest CT with contrast every 6-12 months for 2 years, then annually until year 5 [THYM-3]
All other thymic carcinoma patients: Chest CT with contrast every 3-6 months for 2 years, then annually until year 5 [THYM-3]
imaging_strategy: Chest CT with contrast is the primary imaging modality for surveillance [THYM-3]

MRI is an appropriate alternative to CT in certain clinical situations, including if patients cannot tolerate contrast or to decrease radiation in young patients who will be screened for many years [35, footnote m]
FDG-PET/CT (skull base to mid-thigh) as clinically indicated for initial evaluation [THYM-1]
FDG-PET/CT includes skull-base to mid-thigh [footnote p, THYM-4]
Chest MRI with and without contrast as clinically indicated [THYM-1]
Detection of thymic malignancy versus thymic cyst or thymic hyperplasia can be better discriminated with chest MRI compared to chest CT [36]
laboratory_monitoring: Serum beta-hCG, AFP as appropriate (to rule out germ cell tumors) [THYM-1]
CBC, platelets as part of initial evaluation [THYM-1]
DPYD testing before fluoropyrimidine therapy per NCCN Colon Cancer Guidelines [179, footnote g]
supportive_follow_up: Clinical evaluation for signs of myasthenia gravis and other paraneoplastic syndromes [THYM-1, THYM-3]
Pulmonary function tests as clinically indicated [THYM-1]
Distress assessment using NCCN Distress Thermometer and Problem List [THYM-1, footnote c]
Assessment for treatment-related adverse events based on therapies received
Duration, frequency, and type of imaging for surveillance have not been prospectively evaluated [footnote n]

special_situations.key_message: Approximately one-third of patients with thymomas have myasthenia gravis, the most common paraneoplastic syndrome [126,129,130]. This requires preoperative neurological management before surgical resection.
special_situations.management: Patients with thymoma should be evaluated clinically for signs of myasthenia gravis. If present, patients should receive treatment by a neurologist with experience in myasthenia gravis before undergoing surgical resection [131-133, THYM-1]. Symptoms suggestive of myasthenia gravis include ptosis, diplopia, drooling, proximal muscle weakness, hoarseness, and/or dyspnea.
special_situations.evidence: Approximately one-third of patients with thymomas have myasthenia gravis [126,129,130]. The other paraneoplastic syndromes (hypogammaglobulinemia, pure red cell aplasia) occur in <10% of patients [130].

special_situations.situation: Use of PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitors in thymoma
special_situations.key_message: PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitor therapy is not recommended for patients with thymoma due to concerns about immune-related adverse events in a population predisposed to paraneoplastic autoimmune disorders [180,181].
special_situations.management: Avoid PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitors in thymoma. Among thymoma patients receiving pembrolizumab in a phase 2 trial, 71% (5/7) experienced grade ≥3 immune-related adverse events, including myocarditis [182].
special_situations.evidence: Phase 2 trial demonstrated 71% grade ≥3 immune-related adverse events with pembrolizumab in thymoma [182]. Fatal adverse events with anti-PD-1 therapy have been reported in thymoma patients [180].

special_situations.situation: Use of PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitors in thymic carcinoma
special_situations.key_message: There is concern for a higher rate of immune-related adverse events (eg, myocarditis) with PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitor therapy in thymic carcinoma than seen in most other malignancies. Grade 3-4 myocarditis reported in 5%-9% of patients receiving pembrolizumab [footnote d, THYM-C].
special_situations.management: Pembrolizumab is listed as a preferred subsequent therapy for thymic carcinoma with careful monitoring for immune-related adverse events. See NCCN Guidelines for the Management of Immunotherapy-Related Toxicities. Not recommended in neoadjuvant setting [footnote e].
special_situations.evidence: Response rate of 22.5% for pembrolizumab in thymic carcinoma but 15% severe immune-related adverse events [201,204]. Grade 3-4 myocarditis in 5%-9% of patients [182,201].

special_situations.key_message: Concurrent chemoradiation has demonstrated significant activity in unresectable thymic epithelial tumors, with objective response rates ranging from 50% to 87.5% [98,99,101].
special_situations.management: Treatment options include definitive RT, concurrent chemoradiation, systemic therapy, or observation. A definitive dose of 60-66 Gy is recommended for unresectable disease [98,100]. Carboplatin/paclitaxel and PE can be used with RT as definitive concurrent chemoradiation [footnote b, THYM-C 1 of 3].
special_situations.evidence: Prospective phase 2 study in 56 patients showed 85.7% objective response rate with concurrent chemoradiation [98]. Another study showed 50% overall response rate [99].

special_situations.situation: Preoperative systemic therapy for potentially resectable disease
special_situations.key_message: If R0 resection is considered uncertain, preoperative systemic therapy should be considered to reduce tumor size and increase odds of complete resection [61-64, THYM-2].
special_situations.management: Preoperative systemic therapy (typically combination chemotherapy) may be used for locally advanced disease when complete resection is uncertain. The pooled rate of response to preoperative therapy was 59%; 5-year overall survival was 87% [64].
special_situations.evidence: Meta-analysis showed 59% pooled response rate to preoperative therapy and 87% 5-year OS [64]. Response rates of 70%-80% in largest studies; complete resection achieved in approximately 50% [118,119].

special_situations.key_message: Minimally invasive procedures for thymectomy may be considered if all oncologic principles can be met as in standard of care open thymectomy, and if performed in specialized centers by surgeons with experience in these techniques [THYM-A, 1-15].
special_situations.management: VATS or robot-assisted thoracoscopic surgery may be appropriate for selected patients. A systematic review reported similar 5-year OS (VATS 83%-100% vs open 79%-98%) and 10-year RFS (VATS 89%-100% vs open 80%-93%) [85].
special_situations.evidence: Multiple retrospective studies and systematic reviews support safety and comparable oncologic outcomes of minimally invasive approaches [71-84,85-87]. RATS was associated with lower odds of incomplete resection (OR 0.227; P<.001) in propensity-matched analysis [89].

special_situations.key_message: Octreotide (including LAR) with or without prednisone may be used as subsequent therapy for thymoma if octreotide scan or dotatate PET/CT is positive [13,14, THYM-C 2 of 3].
special_situations.management: Nuclear medicine scan (octreotide scan or dotatate PET/CT, preferred if available) should be performed to assess for octreotide-avid disease [footnote i]. If positive, octreotide ± prednisone may be considered.
special_situations.evidence: Eastern Cooperative Oncology Group Phase II Trial evaluated octreotide alone or with prednisone in advanced thymoma and thymic carcinoma [13].

special_situations.key_message: Patients with certain genetic variants of DPYD that lead to dihydropyrimidine dehydrogenase (DPD) deficiency may be at increased risk of serious and life-threatening toxicities when treated with fluorouracil or capecitabine [179].
special_situations.management: For information regarding DPYD testing, see the NCCN Guidelines for Colon Cancer [footnote g, THYM-C 2 of 3]. This applies to capecitabine and gemcitabine/capecitabine regimens.
special_situations.evidence: FDA notification regarding DPYD variants and fluoropyrimidine toxicity [179].

clinical_pearls: Completeness of resection is the most important prognostic factor for thymic malignancies: 10-year survival with complete resection was 76% versus 28% with incomplete resection or biopsy [12,56-60].
Avoid transpleural biopsy if thymoma is suspected - this risks converting a stage I thymoma to stage IV by seeding tumor within the pleural space [THYM-A, 132,138].

PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitors are not recommended in thymoma (71% grade ≥3 immune-related adverse events including myocarditis) and carry higher risk of myocarditis in thymic carcinoma (5%-9% grade 3-4) compared to other malignancies [180-182].
Bilateral phrenic nerve resection should be avoided during thymectomy due to severe respiratory morbidity [THYM-A].

Thymic carcinomas are distinct from thymomas with different immunohistochemical and genetic features, more aggressive behavior, and different treatment approaches including systemic therapy options [1,2,18,188].
When assessing a mediastinal mass, chest MRI is superior to CT for discriminating thymic malignancy from thymic cyst or hyperplasia, potentially avoiding unnecessary thymectomy [36].
Minimally invasive thymectomy is acceptable if performed at specialized centers with experienced surgeons and if all oncologic principles of open thymectomy can be met [THYM-A, 71-84].
Patients with thymoma have increased risk for second malignancies, although no particular screening studies are currently recommended [3,4,183,184].

All patients should be treated by a multidisciplinary team with experience in thymomas and thymic carcinomas, including thoracic surgeons, radiation oncologists, medical oncologists, neurologists, pathologists, and diagnostic imaging specialists [THYM-2].

Postoperative surveillance duration differs between thymoma (through year 10) and thymic carcinoma (through year 5), with thymic carcinoma requiring more frequent imaging (every 3-6 months vs 6-12 months for the first 2 years) [THYM-3, THYM-4].
